title: Intellectual Property Rights and Debt Financing
abstract: I examine how the investment and financing of innovation are affected by the contractual allocation of intellectual property rights using a Federal Circuit ruling that strengthened firms’ property rights to employee patents. I find that treatment firms’ total debt-to-assets ratio and R&D spending increase by 18% and 9%, respectively, as the residual control over patents increases firms’ incentives to innovate. These effects are more pronounced when ex ante holdup exposure is high. Furthermore, I find a positive marginal effect of asset complementarity as it limits the decline in employee incentives. Consistently, I show that firms’ ex post asset complementarity improves.

article.1. Empirical Strategy:
JEL D23 - Organizational Behavior; Transaction Costs; Property Rights G32 - Financing Policy; Financial Risk and Risk Management; Capital and Ownership Structure; Value of Firms; Goodwill O32 - Management of Technological Innovation and R&D O34 - Intellectual Property and Intellectual Capital Issue Section: Article Editor: Holger Mueller Holger Mueller Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

Corporate inventor-employees are accountable for 90% of all patentable inventions in the United States (Pisegna-Cook 1994;Gruner 2006). The allocation of patent ownership between firms and employees is determined by invention assignment agreements. These contracts are inherently incomplete given highly complex, uncertain, and long innovation processes and, as a result, induce holdup problems and inefficient investment decisions. Under such contractual friction, the allocation of patent ownership is important as the residual control over assets affects the firm’s and employees’ incentives to innovate (Hart 1995;Grossman and Hart 1986). Despite the key underlying contractual friction between the firm and its employees, how the legal environment interacts with holdup problems and affects corporate innovation incentives have been relatively unexplored. To this end, this paper empirically examines how the investment and financing of innovation are affected by the contractual allocation of intellectual property rights and legal restrictions on the ability to write those contracts.

More specifically, this paper aims to show how a court ruling that strengthened firms’ property rights to employee patents changes firms’ incentives to innovate and the demand for credit to finance innovation. This empirical setting not only helps us study how the legal environment shapes corporate innovation through the contractual relationship between firm and employees but also overcomes the identification challenge, where property rights may endogenously arise and are correlated with firms’ innovation and financing decisions. To this end, I exploit a Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit (Federal Circuit) ruling in 2008, which,de facto, shifted patent property rights from inventor-employees to firms in eight pro-employee invention assignment states.1The main regression relies on state-level variation in property rights enforcement through invention assignment agreements in a difference-in-differences setting. The regression estimate captures an increase in the innovation and credit demand of the treated firms in the eight states relative to the innovation and credit demand of the control firms after the Federal Circuit ruling.

I first examine how the Federal Circuit ruling that strengthens firm-ownership of employee patents affects firms’ demand for credit as the residual control over patents would increase firm incentives to invest in innovation. I find that, relative to control firms, treatment firms increase their total debt-to-assets ratio by about 2.5 percentage points, which is about a $60 million increase in total debt. A visual inspection of the treatment effect over time shows no differential trend in the total debt-to-assets ratio between treatment and control firms prior to the 2008 Federal Circuit ruling. Furthermore, the absence of the treatment effect in a falsification test using nonpatenting firms ensures that the empirical setting identifies the causal effect of strengthening firms’ property rights on the demand for credit.

I then explore additional property rights implications on the types of credit demanded by treatment firms. Specifically, I test whether firms demand more secured financing as the increasing returns from innovation likely prompt firms to use more patents as collateral and help ease access to debt financing (Loumioti 2015;Mann 2018;Chava et al. 2017). Consistent with this prediction, I find that treatment firms pledge 45% more patents as collateral when their property rights to employee patents strengthen. Although the pledged patents cannot be directly linked to the amount of secured financing, I additionally find that treatment firms use 23% more bank debt, 55% more secured loans, and 29% more long-term debt, whereas there are no statistically significant changes in unsecured loans and short-term debts. These findings underscore the important property rights effect on lenders’ willingness to supply credit.

Next, I focus on how property rights affect the underlying holdup problem, which is the key contractual friction investigated in the paper, and firms’ subsequent investments in innovation. An underinvestment problem arises when contracts are incomplete as holdup problems reduce investment incentives. Hence, stronger firm ownership of patents post-treatment would reduce firms’ exposures to employee holdup and induce greater financing and investments in innovation by firms. I consistently find that treatment firms increase research and development (R&D) spending by 9% in conjunction with a 10% increase in patent output relative to control firms. Moreover, these effects are more pronounced when a firm has greater ex ante holdup exposures proxied for by a high employee mobility and coordination.

Lastly, I show how the treatment effects are marginally affected by ex ante conditions under which stronger firm ownership of patents would increase firm incentives to innovate without substantially decreasing employee incentives to innovate. In particular, this analysis helps explain the positive net effect of the property rights shift shown in the main results. I find that when firms’ asset complementarity is high, the adverse effect of property rights shift on employee incentives to innovate is limited and results in a positive marginal treatment effect. In contrast, when the number of essential employees is high, such that the adverse impact of the property rights shift is likely larger, the marginal treatment effect becomes negative. These results convey that the net positive effects on firms’ demand for credit and innovation are in part attributable to the underlying asset complementarity that preserves employee incentives to innovate when property rights shift in a corporate innovation setting. Consistently, I also find that firms’ ex post asset complementarity, as measured by self-citation, rises by 23%.

This paper contributes to a few strands of related literature. Property rights theory shows that transferring ownership of an asset from one party to another has a benefit of encouraging investment by the party acquiring ownership but also a cost of discouraging investment by the party from which the asset was acquired (Hart 1995;Grossman and Hart 1986). This paper finds empirical results consistent with theoretical models of property rights in the context of innovation.Claessens and Laeven (2003)empirically shows in a cross-country setting that firms invest more in intangible assets and result in a better asset allocation when property rights strengthen. Using U.K. firms,Acemoglu et al. (2010)find that the incentives for integration increase for high R&D investment intensity firms as concern for the expropriation of R&D declines. This paper contributes to this literature by focusing specifically on the intersection of the legal environment and innovation in the United States, where innovation is affected by the contractual allocation of property rights between the firm and employees and legal restrictions on those contracts.

This paper also adds to the literature on investment incentives and holdup problems between firms and employees. A noncompete agreement has been the conventionally accepted solution to employee holdup problems by limiting employees’ mobility (Rubin and Shedd 1981;Acemoglu and Pischke 1999;Posner et al. 2004;Starr et al. 2021). This paper rather directly examines the contractual agreement underlying the allocation of intellectual property rights as the source of holdup problems between the firm and employees and shows the impact of this agreement on firm incentives to invest in innovation.Hvide and Jones (2018)is a closely related paper, which finds declining inventor incentives to innovate in a university setting when ownership over inventions shifts from the inventors to universities in Norway. The results in this paper do not necessarily contradict those ofHvide and Jones (2018); rather, they highlight the important institutional differences between firms and universities and convey the role of asset complementarity in corporate innovation.

Lastly, this paper also broadly relates to the literature on debt financing of innovation (Robb and Robinson 2014;Kerr and Nanda 2015). More specifically, I find results that are consistent with prior research, which shows the growth in debt financing of innovation by using patents as collateral (Loumioti 2015;Chava et al. 2017;Hochberg et al. 2017;Mann 2018). At the same time, this paper adds to the literature by showing how stronger property rights also help raise lenders’ willingness to supply credit to innovative firms.
1.1 Preinvention assignment agreements

In this paper, I focus on property rights to patents arranged by a contract written between corporate inventor-employees and their employer firms. A preinvention assignment agreement is an employment contract that obligates an employee to assign to the employer interests in any inventions made or conceived during the employment term (Dratler 1979). This contract is widely used and required to be signed by technical employees, engineers, and researchers (Cherensky 1993;Pisegna-Cook 1994;Mattioli 2011). The scope of preinvention assignment agreements may be sufficiently broad to cover categories of inventions beyond employer-specified inventions and may extend past the terms of employment for a reasonable period. As part of employment contracts, preinvention assignment agreements are governed by state law, and, when present, supersede common laws (i.e., the default rule in the absence of such agreements). Generally, courts honor these agreements.

The common law is clear that the inventions of employees hired to invent belongs to the employers, and the employee’s private inventions, developed on the employee’s own time without using the employer’s resources or business information, belong to the employee.2Between these two extremes, it may be unclear whether employees’ inventions were the intended results of employment. To avoid such uncertainties, employers began using written contracts, which precipitated the widespread adoption of invention assignment agreements by employers. In turn, the prevalence of assignment agreements and the inherent disparity in bargaining power between inventors and employers have led to some reform attempts at the state level (Parker 1984). These are explained in Section1.2.

Recently, preinvention assignment agreements have become widely used throughout an organization regardless of an employee’s likelihood of inventing (Mattioli 2011). For example, Ford has initiated a company-wide innovation challenge, encouraging employees from any part of the business to participate by submitting invention ideas for new products or changes to the company’s existing offerings.3The contest rules require a submission of an invention disclosure form (a pre-invention assignment agreement), which states, “Each entrant will assign and Sponsor will hold exclusive right, title and interest in all inventions or other materials submitted and, in all revenue, profits and Net Proceeds generated as a result of commercialization of a Submission[...].”4The company claims that, from the start of the first challenge in January 2015, more than 4,500 Ford employees have submitted invention ideas and nearly 3,500first-timeinventors have participated. This example illustrates two important aspects of pre-invention assignment agreements. The first is the broad use of the agreement acrossalltypes of employees, and the second is the growing importance of the law’s interpretation of agreements on inventions in the gray area. Recently, however, overwhelming employer claims on employee inventions have raised concerns.5Firms are increasingly taking advantage of the protection provided by preinvention assignment agreements, and, thus, such agreements highlight the significant value of knowledge assets to firms.

There are several advantages to using preinvention assignment agreements in this paper. First, as U.S. companies have moved toward technologies that rely on knowledge generation, contractual agreements for the allocation of knowledge assets are timely and appropriate venue for this study. Second, preinvention assignment agreements are widely used in knowledge-intensive firms and somewhat define the division of patent ownership. Lastly, the existence of a plausible exogenous shock to the interpretation of preinvention assignment contracts helps establish causal inference and quantify the effect of strengthening firm ownership of employee patents.

1.2 Institutional setting

Between the late 1970s and the early 1980s, eight states enacted state legislation6to protect inventor-employees from employers’ abuse of their superior negotiation positions and curtail the scope of overly broad employers’ claims on employee inventions (Gullette 1980;Coolley 1985). The first state statute was enacted in Minnesota in 1977, and all subsequent statutes have content and structure similar to the Minnesota statute, with some variations in provisions (Pisegna-Cook 1994). Passing these statutes helped build momentum for similar federal legislative proposals (although none ultimately passed) and improve employee innovation incentives as substantiated by the increasing number of court cases around that time in which inventors won sizable awards from their employers for profitable inventions.7Whether the statutes directly affected credit supply is less clear as the practice of pledging patents was relatively uncommon in the late 1970s and early 1980s. This is an advantage to the empirical setting as it reduces the concern for the treatment states attracting firms that systematically borrow more and, hence, confounding the main results.

The statutes generally state that inventions for which no employer’s resources were used and which do notdirectlyrelate to the employer’s business and/or result from work by the employee for the employer are unassignable, and that any provision which purports to apply to such an invention isvoidandunenforceable(Gullette 1980). Each state statute aims to clarify the gray area under the common law by stating criteria under which employers can pursue rights to employee inventions.8At the same time, the degree of statute enforceability differs across states depending on how the criteria are read to determine the nexus between the invention and the employer’s business (Internet Appendix B.1shows a detailed example of statutes language and differences among the statutes from state to state).

In 2008, the Federal Circuit made a decision inDDB Technologies LLC v. MLB Advanced Media, LLP9regarding a preinvention assignment agreement case that shifted employee invention property rights from employees to firms, resulting in more pro-employer trends toward invention assignment agreements. The Federal Circuit decision had three main parts. First, although employment contracts are governed by state laws, the Federal Circuit ruled that provisions regarding patent assignment will be regulated under federal law. The significance of this statement is that contracts previously subject to specific state-level statutes and jurisdiction are no longer as controlling and significant since provisions regarding patent assignments will now be regulated under federal law (Hedvat 2011). Second, employers are granted authority over patents when “express” language is provided in employment contracts. This means that making claims over employee inventions would become easier in the presence of expressive terms, such as “agrees to and does hereby grant and assign...,” in invention assignment agreements. The expressive terms put the assignment in the present tense; thus, even without any inventions in the present, future inventions are automatically and immediately assigned to firms, under the court’s interpretation (Baniak and Dawson 2009). Third, since the Federal Circuit has nationwide jurisdiction, and the court categorizedDDB Technologies LLC v. MLB Advanced Media, LLPas a precedential case, the decision affects future preinvention assignment agreement cases.10

Effectively, the enforceability of the pro-employee state statutes was incapacitated by the Federal Circuit ruling in the DDB Technologies case. In turn, inventions are automatically vested to the employer at the moment of creation, and the express language disregards any claimed waiver or legal defenses that attempt to preserve inventor rights to even inventions that were deemed unrelated to the employer’s business at the time. Such a situation is particularly problematic when defining a firm’s business without ambiguity is impossible, as employee responsibilities change and firms’ business evolves and expands over time. As a result, the verdict in the DDB Technologies case seems to have broadened the scope of permissible employers’ claims, which “seemingly enhances the rights of employers” by granting employers not only “authority over patents when express language is provided in an employment contract” but also the right to “bring suit to obtain ownership rights of former employees’ patents that they did not demonstrate interest in or control over for several years” (Hedvat 2011;Baniak and Dawson 2009;Graves 2018).

Commentators and practitioners also describe the DDB Technologies case as a “a strong tool for employers,” “a victory for employers that will strengthen the rights of employers to employee inventions,” “a road-map for employers to obtain successful assignment of employee’s patent rights,” and “a clear benefit to employers.”11The practical influence of the Federal Circuit ruling can be easily found in law firms’ advice to corporate clients at the time, such as recommending that their corporate clients include express phrases in preinvention assignment agreements. For example,

TheDDB Technologiesdecision should provide comfort to employers that the effect of language assigning patents in employment agreements will be interpreted uniformly pursuant to federal law and will not be subject to differing interpretation under varying state law. The decision creates a road-map to which employers can be reasonably certain that if their employment agreements contain language that expressly assigns rights in existing and future inventions, this assignment language will be interpreted under federal law to vest automatically ownership of the inventions with the employer, regardless of the state law governing the agreement or the domicile of the employee.12

In summary, the Federal Circuit ruling effectively strengthened firms’ property rights to employee inventions in the eight formerly pro-employee states by preempting existing state legislation that protected inventor-employee interests regarding invention assignment agreements. In the following section, I explain the implementation of this empirical setting in regression analyses.

1.3 Methodology

I exploit the Federal Circuit ruling in 2008 as an exogenous shock in a difference-in-differences framework. There are a few empirical advantages to this setting. First, although the definition of “invention” is broad in assignment agreements and state statutes,13the Federal Circuit ruling is mostly applicable to patented inventions because only patent cases are treated as a matter of federal law.14Second, Federal Circuit cases are heard by a panel comprising three randomly selected, an approach that minimizes potential political influences. Lastly, Federal Circuit case sessions are generally held in Washington, DC, which further limits the possible impact of local state economies on the Federal Circuit’s ruling.

I define firms with headquarters located in the eight states affected by the Federal Circuit’s decision as treated firms. I use the state in which headquarters are located as the definition of state because “Generally, the state where the employer is located or where the job duties are performed will be a reasonable choice of law and likely be honored” (American Bar Association 2014). I present a few example cases in theInternet Appendix B.2to verify that the headquarter state is indeed a reasonable indicator for treatment state. For all firms, the posttreatment period is defined as the years on and after 2008.

The main regression specification is as follows.

T o t a l d e b t / A s s e t s i , s , t = α + β t r e a t i , s × p o s t t + δ i + γ t + ϵ i , s , t .

Total debt-to-assets captures firm’s credit demand. Other main regression dependent variables include firm innovation measures, such as R&D spending and innovation output. An important identification assumption for the difference-in-differences estimate,β⁠, to be consistent is that, absent treatment, the change in the total debt-to-assets ratio for firms in the treatment states would not have been different than the change in the same ratio for firms in the control states.

Under the identification assumption, the difference-in-differences coefficient captures the additional changes for firms in the treatment states, relative to firms in the control states, following the shift in property rights after the 2008 Federal Circuit decision. All regressions use fully saturated specifications including all interaction terms and stand-alone terms. However, to conserve space, I report only the main interaction terms in the tables. In addition to firm and year fixed effects presented in the above specification, I use firm and industry-year fixed effects to rule out potential unobserved heterogeneity across industries over time and obtain a more precise estimate. Lastly, I include error clustering at the state level to correct for potential error correlation within the same state and account for serial correlations in the dependent variable.
article.2. Hypothesis Development:
2.1 Increasing demand for credit

When contracts in a relationship-specific investment cannot fully specify the usage of the asset in every state of the world, that is, incomplete contracts (Hart 1995;Grossman and Hart 1986), a holdup problem arises from the fear of not getting a sufficient return on the investment and results in an underinvestment. Under incomplete contracts, asset ownership provides residual control over the usage of the asset and, hence, gives the asset owner stronger incentives to make ex ante investments.

In the context of corporate innovation, an invention assignment agreement dictates some patent ownership allocation between a firm and its inventor-employees. However, the ill-defined nature of innovation makes it too costly for firms to write a complete contract that specifies the delivery of an exact innovation or usage in every state of the world (Aghion and Tirole 1994). Therefore, patent ownership, which gives residual control over inventions, becomes the source of power. When the Federal Circuit ruling strengthens firms’ property rights to employee inventions, firm incentives to invest in innovation rise as patent ownership increases firms’ returns from innovation. Therefore, I expect the stronger property rights posttreatment to increase firms’ demand for credit15to fund innovation investments and result in greater borrowing by treatment firms relative to control firms.

2.2 Implications for types of credit

Debt has increasingly become a more viable option for financing innovation as the ability to use patents as collateral, in particular, has helped innovative firms access debt financing (Loumioti 2015;Robb and Robinson 2014;Hochberg et al. 2017;Mann 2018). Hence, stronger property rights to employee patents would affect not only firms’ demand for credit but also their types of borrowing; the increasing returns from innovation would make firms more inclined to use patents as collateral and ease access to secured debt financing by lowering the borrower risk (Giannetti 2003;Diamond 2004;Qian and Strahan 2007). I predict that firms demand more secured and long-term debt financing by pledging patents as collateral.

2.3 Holdup problems and incentives to innovate

An increase in firms’ property rights to employee patents increases firm incentives while likely reducing employee incentives to innovate. Whether this shift results in an overall positive or negative surplus depends onrelativechanges in incentives and marginal productivity for the firm and employees. Hence, I further exploit ex ante conditions under which the firm-ownership structure would increase firm incentives without substantially decreasing employee incentives, and examine how these conditions marginally affect the main results. The extended implications ofHart (1995)highlight that an ownership structure that increases an asset owner’s investment without decreasing the nonowner’s investment moves the parties closer to the first-best and increases the total surplus. In other words, to the extent that the increase in firms’ property rights increases firm incentives more than it hurts inventors’ incentives to innovate, the net effect of this firm-ownership structure can still be positive.

When the degree of asset complementarity is high for firms’ innovation processes, inventors’ marginal returns from innovation are unaffected by whether the firms own all patents or the status quo ownership prevails with inventors’ limited access to firm-owned resources. This is because inventors’ patents alone have limited use without access to firms’ resources when asset complementarity is high. Accordingly, I predict that firms’ greater ex ante asset complementarity marginally increases the treatment effect by limiting the decrease in inventor incentives. On the other hand, when inventors’ human capital is essential, firms’ marginal investment return is not enhanced by strengthening property rights in the absence of inventors’ human capital. The more essential inventors’ human capital is to a firm, the greater the negative impact of the Federal Circuit ruling on their incentives to innovate. Hence, I predict that greater ex ante essential inventor human capital marginally reduces treatment effects. Lastly, I expect that stronger firm ownership of employee patents also reduces ex post holdup problem.
article.3. Data:
Although preinvention assignment agreements are commonly required as part of an employment contract, whether a firm uses the contract is only partially observable.16However, it is widely accepted that pre-invention assignment agreements are typically presented to engineers and almost all technical employees. To ensure that sample firm employees are bound by such contracts, I restrict my sample topatentingU.S. public firms (country of incorporation is the United States) in Compustat. I also exclude financial firms (SIC codes 6000-6999) and utilities (SIC codes 4900-4999) because these firms may be affected by capital requirements or regulatory supervision. I drop observations with missing total assets and replace missing debt values with zero.

Next, I collect patent grant data from the U.S. Patent and Trademark Office (USPTO). USPTO provides U.S. patent grant documents from 1926 to the present. I download each document between 2003 and 2016. Each document contains information about the patent, application and grant dates, names and locations of inventors and assignees, and citations. I keep only the utility patents17assigned to U.S. domicile corporations to ensure that the empirical setting applies to the sample firms. Then, I name-match the collected data to the Compustat sample firms. A detailed description of how the data was collected is in theInternet Appendix A.

The final sample consists of 16,462 firm-year observations over the sample period of 2003 to 2013.Table 1describes the sample firms. The main dependent variable is total debt-to-assets ratio, computed by dividing the sum of short-term and long-term debt by total assets. Panel A describes the financial characteristics of all sample firms during the entire sample period from 2003 to 2013. Note that the total debt-to-assets ratio is slightly smaller than the average of all Compustat firms. This is consistent with the stylized fact that knowledge-intensive firms tend to carry less debt. The ratio is even smaller for firms in the treatment states presented in panel B. Panel B compares the firm characteristics by treatment and control state firms only during the pretreatment period between 2003 and 2007. The treatment state firms are smaller and have relatively lower total debt-to-assets ratios. Moreover, in general, the treatment state firms seem to be involved in slightly more patent activities. Thep-values in the last column show some statistically different firm-level characteristics between the treatment state firms and control state firms.

The fact that the treated and control firms differ on observable dimensions may raise concerns for endogeneity issues in the empirical analyses given the possibility that they may also differ on unobservable dimensions in a way that violates the parallel trends assumption. These concerns are mitigated in the following ways. First, the difference-in-differences setting fully accounts for any observable level difference between the treatment and control groups using thetreatindicator. Second, despite no way to formally test the parallel trends assumption, I show inFigures 1andA1that the treatment and control groups during the pretreatment period seem to show parallel trends. In addition, I include firm fixed effects in all of my regression specifications to mitigate potential confounding effects of the time-invariant unobservables. Lastly, I perform a robustness check with matching on observable regressions at the end of the results section.

article.4. Results:
4.1 Increasing demand for credit

a Invention assignment agreements are incomplete contract, in which patent ownership becomes the source of power by giving residual control over inventions. Therefore, the Federal Circuit ruling that strengthens firm-ownership of employee patents would increase firm incentives to invest in innovation and, as a result, the demand for credit to finance innovations.Table 2reports the main baseline regression results on debt financing. In panel A, the dependent variable is the total debt-to-assets ratio. Column 1 includes firm and year fixed effects to remove firm-specific time-invariant effects and aggregate time trends. Column 2 uses a more stringent specification by additionally including the industry-year fixed effects to absorb potential industry-year-specific shocks. The difference-in-differences estimates are similar. Following the Federal Circuit ruling, which shifted employee patent property rights to firms, treatment firms’ total debt increases by about 2.5 percentage points relative to control firms. This is an 18% increase in the total debt-to-assets ratio for an average treated firm with $2.43 billion in pretreatment total assets and a total debt-to-assets ratio of 0.14. This estimate translates into about a $60 million increase in total debt.18

Figure 1traces the estimates for the difference-in-differences coefficient over the years to treatment. Notably, prior to the treatment int=0⁠, the coefficients are statistically indistinguishable from zero and only become positive and statistically significant following the 2008 Federal Circuit ruling. This graphical illustration provides some visual inspection of parallel trends, where the flat line prior to the treatment is suggestive of no preexisting differential trends in the total debt-to-assets ratio (a similar figure forLTDissuanceis presented inFigure A1). The graph also emphasizes the sharp change in debt financing for treatment firms relative to control firms as the impact of the ruling was swift and easily perceptible for firms. The implications and significance of who owns employee inventions are evident in media discussions.20Following the Federal Circuit decision, law firms promptly informed and advised their corporate clients that the ruling’s new interpretation of existing invention assignment agreements favored firms’ rights (see Section1.2). This immediate increase in demand for credit is attributable in part to firms’ existing patents.21Additionally, the slight increase in the treatment effect in Year 4 likely captures some lagged effects of financing from the increase in returns from innovation as firms increase innovation investment, which further reinforces firms’ ability to access secured financing.

The falsification test inTable 3further helps establish the internal validity of the empirical setting. To ensure that the Federal Circuit ruling is only relevant for patenting firms’ debt financing through its impact on invention assignment agreements, I rerun the baseline regressions inTable 2using onlynonpatentingfirms. The coefficients for the total debt-to-assets ratio are statistically insignificant. The magnitudes are also substantially smaller for nonpatenting firms, which have a higher average pretreatment total debt-to-assets ratio of 0.28. The coefficients for long-term debt issuance are even slightly negative and statistically indistinguishable from zero. Overall, the results in this section ensure that the main regressions identify the causal effect of increasing the property rights of patenting firms after the Federal Circuit ruling.

4.2 Implications on types of credit

I further examine whether strengthening firms’ property rights has implications on the demand for certain types of credit. Firm ownership of employee patents increases returns from innovation and helps ease access to secured or long-term credit by prompting firms to use patents as collateral (Loumioti 2015;Robb and Robinson 2014;Hochberg et al. 2017;Mann 2018), which is consistent with the increasing credit demand. To this end, I collect patent assignment data from USPTO and identify patent reassignments marked as “security interest” to verify whether firms make greater use of patents as collateral posttreatment.22

Table 4presents the results. The dependent variable for the first four columns is the number of pledged patents scaled by the pre-treatment average total debt. The coefficient is translated as pledging about two additional patents for an average firm with $470 million in total debt. The last two columns estimate Poisson regressions using the number of pledged patents as the outcome variable. The coefficient in column 5 shows that the increase in firms’ property rights increases the number of pledged patents by about 45% for treatment firms relative to control firms. The results become stronger, partly as Poisson regression estimates the treatment effect conditional on having any patents pledged during the sample period.

To further emphasize the implications of property rights for firms’ access to secured financing, I use DealScan loan data consisting of bank loans only.23I differentiate loan types into secured and unsecured to provide further supporting evidence that shows strengthening of firms’ property rights increases not only returns from innovation but also firms’ ability to access secured borrowing. InTable 5, panel B, I find consistent results. The dependent variable in columns 1 through 4 is the amount of secured (unsecured) loans, which is computed by summing the facility-level loan amounts. As expected, I find that the amount ofsecuredloans increases by one percentage point, which is equivalent to a 55% increase, and a $200,000 increase in secured loans. In contrast, changes in the amount ofunsecuredloans remain statistically insignificant.24As a corollary, I also examine the impact of property rights on the cost of debt using the natural logarithm of all-in-drawn spread, followingChava and Roberts (2008), as the dependent variable in columns 5 through 8. The coefficients for the secured loan interest rates are indeed negative but statistically insignificant. Hence, strengthening the property rights of firms appears to facilitate greater borrowing, mostly in amount rather than from cheaper costs.

The results in this section collectively convey the property rights implications consistent with both the greater demand and supply of credit. The growing incentives to innovate and the greater patent output appear to encourage firms to use more patents as collateral, which further increase lenders’ willingness to lend (Giannetti 2003;Diamond 2004;Qian and Strahan 2007).25

4.3 Holdup problem and incentives to innovate

In relationship-specific investments, such as corporate innovation, an underinvestment problem arises when contracts are incomplete as a holdup problem reduces investment incentives. In this section, I further explore how strengthening property rights affects firms’ investment incentives and the use of new capital in innovation and how the underlying contractual friction changes after the Federal Circuit ruling.

First, I examine changes in R&D-related investments following the new capital infusion shown in Section4.1. In the context of the Federal Circuit ruling that shifted property rights from employee-inventors to firms, increasing firm spending on employee compensation seems to be the most effective investments to maintain employee incentives to innovate. Unfortunately, such data does not exist, and identifying employees as “inventors” presents a challenge. Therefore, I turn to the next best proxies and use the total R&D expense and investments in plants and equipment.Table 6reports the results.26In panel A, columns 1 through 4 use level changes, and columns 5 through 8 use R&D expense per inventor to further control for the pretreatment scale of innovation.27After the Federal Circuit ruling, treatment firms increase R&D spending by about 9%, or $28 million for an average firm with about 230 inventor-employees, relative to control firms. Panel B shows that firms also spend 6% more in physical assets to complement the increased investment in innovation.

I further show how strengthening intellectual property rights interacts with the underlying holdup problem. The benefit of the Federal Circuit ruling is the reduction in firms’ exposure to employee holdup, and, hence, the demand-for-credit effect would be larger for firms exposed more to the holdup problem ex ante. I first estimate the marginal treatment effects conditioning on the ex ante degree of holdup.Table 8, panel A, columns 1 through 4, presents the results. I use two proxies for the degree of ex ante holdup. The first is noncompete agreement (NCA) enforcement (Garmaise 2011), where a lower index score indicates weaker enforcement of a noncompete agreement, and therefore higher employee mobility and greater ex ante employee holdup risk (columns 1 and 2). The second proxy is the average number of inventors assigned per patent, which intends to capture costly coordination (Merges 2009) and multidimensional holdup problems (columns 3 and 4). Consistent with the prediction, the results show that firms in the low NCA enforcement states and with higher potential coordination problems have positive marginal treatment effects.

Next, I examine how the treatment effects interact with changes in employee incentives when property rights shift.Table 8, panel A, columns 5 through 8, presents the results. Firms benefit from the Federal Circuit ruling as it curtails firms’ exposure to employee holdup; at the same time, the ruling likely reduces inventor incentives to innovate. The net effect, however, is determined by therelativechanges in both the firms’ and employees’ incentives. That is, a relatively larger increase in firm incentives and marginal productivity would explain the net positive effects shown in Sections4.1and4.2. To gauge exactly how the Federal Circuit ruling results in a netincreasein innovation, I use ex ante cross-sectional heterogeneity in conditions that limits/amplify the decrease in inventor incentives following the Federal Circuit ruling and examine how the different conditions marginally affect the treatment effects. From this exercise, one can infer what may be driving the net positive effects of Federal Circuit rulings on firm innovation.31

Asset complementarity would preserve nonowners’ incentives to invest (Hart 1995) as inventor patents alone have a limited effect on inventors’ marginal returns from innovation without access to firm resources, including firm patents. I use self-citation as the proxy for firm asset complementarity. I find in columns 5 and 6 in panel A ofTable 8that a higher asset complementarity as measured by self-citations results in more profound marginal treatment effects. In contrast, one should expect to find negative marginal treatment effect when firms’ innovation productivity is adversely affected by a disincentivized inventor, who provides essential human capital. I first compute the inventor-level technology proximity (Jaffe 1986) between any patents developed by a given inventor and the firm’s remaining patents and define an inventor as “essential” to the firm’s innovation if the technology proximity is high. I then calculate the firm-level technology proximity as the fraction of inventors who are essential. I find in columns 7 and 8 that a larger fraction of essential inventors substantially reduces the treatment effects. In panel B, I also show that the marginal effects of ex ante degree of holdup, asset complementarity, and essential inventor human capital also consistently explain firms’ patenting activity and firms’ ability to pledge patents.

Finally, I explore whether shifting property rights to firms reduces ex post holdup problems. Self-citation captures the degree of change in firms’ complementary use of patents, and the positive and statistically significant coefficient is suggestive of an increase in firms’ knowledge accumulation and incentives to internalize knowledge spillovers created by firms’ own developments (Hall et al. 2001). InTable 9, panel A, the first four columns show that the number of self-citations increases by about 23%. The treatment effect strengthens somewhat when using the Poisson regression, as shown in column 6. Similarly, in panel B, there is greater collaboration among inventor-employees, proxied for by the average number of inventor-employees assigned per patent, around the time of the Federal Circuit ruling. Inventor collaboration increases by about 4%, where the pretreatment unconditional mean is 2.77 inventors per patent.32

article.5. Robustness:
5.1 Matching regressions

A common concern in the difference-in-differences framework is whether the treatment is reasonably exogenous. Despite statistically significant differences between treatment and control firms (Table 1), visual inspections of parallel trends (Figures 1andA1) ensure that the pretreatment differences between the treatment and control groups are not likely driving the results. Nonetheless, to further ease concerns regarding time-varying differences in observable firm characteristics, I rerun the main regressions using matched samples on observable firm characteristics and industry. As with any endogeneity problems, a matching regression itself does not fully resolve identification concerns, but, used in conjunction with the difference-in-differences setting, can provide a useful robustness test for earlier regression results.

The propensity score is constructed from estimating probit regressions and matching firms on four-digit SIC industry codes. To ensure that the matching process embodies the parallel trends assumption of the difference-in-differences framework, I include the annual growth rate of various outcome variables in the propensity score (Lemmon and Roberts 2010). The matching process performs the most stringent one-to-one nearest-neighbor matching with replacement on a common support of the estimated propensity score.

Table 10, panel A, reports the propensity score matching diagnostics. The prematch columns show the observable and statistically significant differences between the treatment and control groups in the pretreatment period. It is important to note that the differences in growth rates of various outcome variables between the treatment and control groups are statistically insignificant, reinforcing the parallel trends assumptions. The next three columns compare the same variablesafterthe propensity score matching. Thep-values reported on the pairwise mean differences between the treatment and control groups all become statistically insignificant, ensuring that the matching process has removed meaningful differences on observable dimensions. In sum, the main results of this paper remain robust to matching away observable differences.

5.2 State-level heterogeneity

Lerner and Seru (2022)emphasizes the regional biases of patent data, particularly for California and Massachusetts, where innovative activities are concentrated. Although California is just one of the eight treatment states in the empirical setting of this paper, a large proportion of sample firms are indeed located in California. Therefore, to ensure that the findings are still robust without California, I reestimate the main regressions by each treatment state. The results are reported inTable A2and show that the main results are not solely driven by California. Furthermore, although California presents the most robust results across all regressions, it is not always the state with the most pronounced treatment effect.

This exercise further highlights some state-level heterogeneity in the underlying state statutes. As described inInternet Appendix Section B.1, although the enactment years are somewhat spread out across the eight treatment states, the content and structure of the state statutes are similar to the first one enacted by Minnesota in 1977. There remain certain distinctions, largely between the Minnesota/California and the Washington/North Carolina statues, concerning the nexus between the invention and the employer’s business, such that Minnesota and California allowed only directly connected inventions to be pursued by employers. This distinction, to some degree, highlights some variations in the results between Minnesota/California and Washington/North Carolina. The demand for credit is higher for previously more pro-employee statute states, such as Minnesota/California, and their patenting activity is lower. The opposite is true for Washington/North Carolina. The underlying inventor incentives can provide some explanation. Since the degree of shift in property rights is relatively greater in formerly pro-employee Minnesota/California, firms would need greater financing to invest in and maintain inventor incentives to innovate. In contrast, previously and relatively more pro-employer Washington/North Carolina would still benefit from the increase in innovative activities, while their demand for financing would be somewhat lower.

5.3 Financial crisis

The fact that the Federal Circuit ruling overlaps with the 2008 financial crisis raises concern that some state-level heterogeneous reactions to the financial crisis may be driving the results. Ideally, one would include state×year fixed effects in the regressions to control for the time-varying state-level confounders. However, state×year fixed effects would also absorb the main treat×post effect. Therefore, I address this concern in two alternative ways. First, I show that the state-level economic conditions do not differentially change over the financial crisis period. Second, I exploit Nevada’s passage of state legislation in 2001, which is similar to the spirit of the 2008 Federal Circuit ruling.

InTable A3, I show that differences in the means of important state-level economic variable growth rates between treated and untreated states were statistically indistinguishable around 2008. I show that gross domestic product (GDP) growth, GDP per capita growth, and unemployment rate growth are all statistically indifferent from zero for all years during the sample period, ensuring that trends in the state economic conditions of the treatment and control states are similar both before and after 2008. Hence, concerns regarding heterogeneous state-level responses to the financial crisis can be safely ruled out. The falsification result is also useful in ruling out the concern that innovative firms, particularly in California, may have benefited more from low interest rates during the financial crisis. For this condition to drive the results, I should also find similar results on debt financing in the nonpatenting firm sample. This is not the case. Furthermore, there seems to be no logical explanation why the interest rates in these specific eight treatment states are differentially affected.

In 2001, the Nevada legislature passed a law stating that “an employer is the sole owner of any patentable invention or trade secret developed by his employee during the course of the employment that relates directly to work performed during the course of employment.”34La France (2002)describes Nevada’s statute in contrast to the eight treatment state statutes, which “enhance, rather than curtail, the rights of employed inventors...by typically limiting the enforceability of preinvention assignment agreements.” Hence, Nevada’s statute is, in essence, similar to the Federal Circuit ruling that strengthened firms’ property rights to employee inventions. Accordingly, I expect to find similar results using Nevada as an empirical setting. I employ the same difference-in-differences framework between 1996 and 2006 and use firms in Nevada as the treatment group.Table A4reports the results, which are consistent with the main results using the Federal Circuit ruling in 2008, except for the number of patents scaled by R&D expense, which is negative and significant.

Interpretations of the Nevada results, however, should be considered with caution. First, Nevada’s adoption of a pro-employer statute was the result of deliberate efforts to attract more high-technology businesses to Nevada (La France 2002). This raises concerns about lobbying and the selection of firms that would have benefited from such corporate actions. Second, to the extent that Nevada’s law deprives employees of their common law rights by allowing an employer to claim invention rights without their consent (La France 2002), the adverse impact on employee incentives would have been much greater, which likely explains the negative coefficient for the number of patents scaled by R&D expense. According toLa France (2002)andRavid (2013), Nevada’s law discouraged worker immigration to Nevada and led workers to choose California or Washington over Nevada. Therefore, despite these caveats, the Nevada results offer some assurance that the main results in Section4are not driven by unobservable factors related to the financial crisis.

5.4 Alternative explanations

If firms have geographic subsidiaries located in multiple states and thus are exposed to multiple state laws governing invention assignment agreements, the effect of the treatment may be less clear.Table A5shows that this is not the case. Each column presents baseline regression estimates for zero, fewer than or equal to one, or fewer than or equal to two geographic subsidiaries, respectively.35The results still hold, but the statistical significance weakens, a shift that is possibly attributable to the significant reduction in the number of observations.

I avoid including time-varying control variables that may be affected by the treatment and give inconsistent estimates of the treatment effect. However, inTable A6, I also show that the main results remain robust to controlling for the common determinants of debt, such as size, age, and profitability in columns 1 through 3, and for firms’ ex ante innovation level by including patent stock and R&D expense in columns 4 and 5.

Next, since firms can choose their headquarter state,36I cannot completely rule out the possibility that the results may be affected by factors that are correlated with both headquarter location and financing decisions. For example, innovative firms may select out of treatment states, leaving high tangibility firms with easier access to debt. Alternatively, innovative firms with greater investment opportunities may select into treatment states to materialize their investment opportunities through debt financing or to benefit from a state’s corporate tax.37To rule out these concerns, I further control for state-tax, asset tangibility, and investment opportunities in columns 6 through 8, and find that the results remain robust. The regression results of other outcome variables by including the same set of control variables are reported inTable A7. All the results are robust to the concerns addressed in this section.
article.6. Discussion:
The main results do not necessarily indicate that inventor incentives to innovate increase following the Federal Circuit ruling. Nonetheless, the net positive results in Section4convey that there must be some offsetting forces that limit the change in inventor incentives to innovate. This section explores a few nonmutually exclusive, likely explanations and provides supporting empirical results when possible.

The first possible explanation involves asset complementarity and firms’ comparative advantage. In an untabulated result, I find a statistically indistinguishable change in inventor-level productivity following the Federal Circuit ruling.38This is broadly consistent with the theory that complementarity should yield benefits but no costs because marginal return from owning part of an asset are the same as marginal returns from not owning any assets (Hart 1995).39In addition, firms’ comparative advantage—that is, an increase in firms’ marginal productivity from increasing investments—may be sufficient to offset the decrease in inventor incentives. This explanation also seems plausible given that firms have comparative advantages in collectively managing, commercializing, and providing resources for innovation (Gruner 2006).

Second, firms may have chosen to increase inventor compensation to maintain inventor incentives to innovate. Considering the relationship-specificity of corporate innovation, increasing inventor compensation would have been cheaper than buying nonspecific technology in the market. A direct test of this hypothesis requires detailed inventor-employee-level wages and inventor labor market data, which are not available.40However, there are anecdotal stories about firms gradually increasing invention-related compensation to incentivize employees. For example, since Ford Motor Company initiated the firm’s internal innovation competition by encouraging employees from any division or rank to invent, the company has also introduced new compensation measures for Ford inventors. Employees with promising ideas can receive a 3 month membership to TechShop, which provides equipment and space for inventors to turn ideas into prototypes. More importantly, employees are given financial incentives, including a share of license income, and other monetary compensations.

Although a detailed test is not possible because of the lack of firm-level inventor-employee compensation data, I provide a state-level analysis of the wages of innovation-related occupations inTable A8. I obtain occupation-level wages by state over the sample period from the Bureau of Labor Statistics Occupational Employment Statistics (OES). In columns 1 and 2, I use a coarse classification of occupation codes that are potentially closely related to R&D and inventions. In columns 3 and 4, I further narrow the data to subclassifications of occupation codes that explicitly mention “research and development” and/or “invention.” Panel A shows a correlation that the average annual wages of occupation profiles related to R&D and innovation in treatment states increase by about $1,200 (column 3).41Although a firm-level analysis is not feasible, because the treatment is at the state level and the occupation code further helps narrow the results to potentially affected employees, the results add some insights into understanding the main findings in Section4.2.

Lastly, I explore state-levelaggregateinnovation to substantiate if the increase in state-wages of occupations in R&D (Table A8) is associated with the similar increase in innovation output after the Federal Circuit ruling. I use the universe of patents on USPTO patent grant records, which include public and private firms, government, and individuals. InTable A8, panel B, columns 1 through 3, I confirm a general property rights effect on state-level aggregate innovation. In columns 4 through 6, I further divide the state-level sample into different assignee groups. Columns 4 and 5 capture state-level aggregate innovation effects only on firms and government, where the tension from shifting property rights to employee patents exists. Despite the group-specific effects, the estimates are positive and statistically significant, showing that the increase in property rights to patents boosts both firm and government innovation. In contrast, the estimate in column 6 is small and statistically insignificant, as individual inventors should not have been affected by the Federal Circuit ruling. Subgroup analyses help validate the empirical setting at the state level, where the Federal Circuit ruling is only relevant for subgroups that experience tension in patent ownership using invention assignment agreements and employee innovation incentives are likely held up by higher wages.
article.7. Conclusion:
This paper examines how corporate innovation is affected by an underlying contractual friction and a legal environment governing the agreement that specifies the allocation of intellectual property rights between a firm and its employees. Using the Federal Circuit ruling on invention assignment agreements as an exogenous shock that strengthens firms’ property rights to employee patents, this paper finds an increase in firm incentives to innovate, which is shown by increases in both the demand for credit and subsequent R&D investment and output. These effects are stronger when firms are more exposed to employee holdup ex ante. Furthermore, this paper explores how the underlying holdup problem is affected by the Federal Circuit ruling. The paper presents supporting evidence on increasing asset complementarity post-treatment and further highlight the important role of asset complementarity in limiting reduction in employee incentives to innovate, which contributes to the net positive effects of strengthening firm ownership of employee patents.

The results in this paper collectively showcase an important contribution toward understanding how intellectual property rights affect firms’ financial decisions, underlying contractual frictions, and innovation. This is an important and timely question in corporate innovation as the United States has become a more knowledge-based economy and firms have moved away from technologies that rely on brick-and-mortar assets and toward those that rely on the generation of knowledge assets.

13 The state statutes do not precisely define the term “invention” despite the very broad scope of inventions covered by assignment agreements, including improvements, software, processes, ideas, trademarks, and discoveries, whether patentable or copyrightable or not ( Cherensky 1993 ). However, Gullette (1980) argues that the term “invention” should be interpreted to cover only those inventions associated with patentable subject matter. Moreover, since patent rights exclude others from making, using, or selling the invention, employers would be most interested in pursuing economically profitable inventions that are already patented or can be patented. Some also describe preinvention assignment agreements as typically requiring the assignment of ownership of any patented product or process ( Bartow 1997 ).

14 Hedvat (2011) also emphasizes that “Although patents are governed by federal law, other areas of intellectual property may not be subject to the same treatment. 15 I focus on debt as the main financing outcome. Despite the pecking order theory, earlier studies have shown that innovative firms rely on equity financing as the extremely skewed return distribution of innovative investments and the lack of access to collateral make these firms credit constrained ( Falato et al. 2020 ; Brown et al. 2013 ; Brown et al. 2009 ). However, these financial frictions are lesser concern for knowledge-intensive public firms that are not in the early-stage innovation. In untabulated result, I confirm statistically insignificant changes in firms’ equity issuance. More recently, these firms have been shown to increasingly rely on the signaling and collateral value of patents, which make debt a viable source of financing for innovation (see Section 2.2 ).

18 The economic magnitude of the estimates is as large as that of improved creditor rights. Bae and Goyal (2009) finds that better enforceability of loan contracts increases loan amounts by $57 million. Loumioti (2015) documents that firms using patents as collateral increase secured syndicated loan amounts by $51 million. Mann (2018) finds that strengthening creditor rights increases total debt by $26 million per quarter. Since firm size is right-skewed, a perhaps more relevant statistics is a $4.85 million increase in total debt for the median firm size of $195 million. This is comparable to the median value of patents found in Kogan et al. (2017) .

27 The number of inventors is approximated from counting the number of unique inventor names from the patent documents collected from USPTO. This poses a few limitations. First, patent documents account for inventors on only the eventually granted patents. Second, the inventor to employer match is not perfectly time sensitive. Third, since the counting of unique inventors relies on inventor names, the approach gives some room for error. However, such error is not likely to be systematic or to affect the regression estimates.

33 To further reinforce the results, I use the synthetic control method, which is a data-driven procedure using a combination of control firms as “a synthetic control” for each treated firm in the sample ( Abadie 2021 ). To ensure that the synthetic control closely matches the characteristics of the treated units, I restrict the donor pool by using the propensity score matched sample. In this way, although a synthetic control procedure alone cannot explicitly match firms on industry, using the restrictive donor pool of firms that are close in industry will ease some concerns. The results using synthetic control are presented in Figure A2 and appear robust and similar in magnitude to the baseline results.

41 It is important to point out important caveats and some limitations in the interpretations. Since this is state-level wage, it includes both private and public firms, of which my sample firms would represent a small fraction. Moreover, wages are determined by labor market conditions, which are not thoroughly reflected in the regression specifications. Since occupation codes are defined by tasks and not by rank or title, the wages include employees of all rank and title and are not limited to corporate inventor-employees. Also, the wages do not include bonuses, which also widely used to compensate inventors for successful patent applications.
